other_observers: Nathan Pieplow, Andy Boyce, David Ely, and Eric Zorawowicz; first found by Brandon Percival
specific_location: Chico Basin Ranch, at the banding station
description: the bird was a thrasher about the same size as the Brown Thrasher we saw at the same location (but never near each other). The bill was slightly but noticeably longer, about the length of the head, and with a bit more of a decurve (which was also slight but noticeable). The bill color overeall was black, with a pale gray base to the lower mandible.  The bird's upperparts were a rufous-brown color, much browner than the nearby Brown Thrasher, but still with a noticeable rufous tinge. The cap was this rufous-brown color, with the cheek being a medium gray, with a bit of the rufous-brown bleeding in around the top and back edges. The iris was bright orange-yellow. The bird had a black and fairly bold malar stripe, and a very faint stripe of white between the malar and the gray cheek patch. The remainder of the upperparts were the same rufous-brown, uniform except for two thin white wingbars and very slightly more rufous in the uppertail coverts.  The bird's underparts were clean white, with very bold black streaks down the length of the underparts, except for the unmarked throat and lower belly. The undertail coverts were also white with some very faint streaks that were only visible in the scope when the bird was close.Behaviors: The bird was mostly perched low in thick brush, hopping around some but most staying still. It would occasionally fly from bush to bush, and also occasionally would scratch on the ground.Call: none heardPlumage: adult
photographer: Photographer: Andrew Spencer, Andrew Spencer, Andrew Spencer
